speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, you were telling us how your father's business continued even though war had started in other parts of Europe. Can you tell me when your family started to sense that things were starting to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it started also to change-- as I-- I think I was-- when they had that revolution. In '38, I think. At that time, the workers of the sawmill in a way they revolt and they thought that they can take possession of everything. And because-- I'm very proud to remember that the family treat the workers like very-- if that's a Yiddish word, [YIDDISH]. It means everybody was there really considered part of that family, because it was the Bronstein family. It wasn't just my father. It was the whole Bronstein family with this operation. The grandfather. And the grandfather had four brothers. I should mention that they had also a son before my father was born. In Europe, they were using some time-- when the first son was born or so, they were using sometimes the maiden name of the mother. Sometime. Not in ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his name, the maiden name of the grandmother from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was Rosner, not Rosen to confused with my grandfather from my mother. Now, Rosner-- and I should mention that because it came to me-- he loved people and children. So those days, he became a delegate to the League of Nation in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when he died, I remember my grandmother, every [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] when it came, his memorial, she used to get from New York a plaque and all the thing. It was all the time in black over there. Because he always said he loved very much my father, Kahn. He want to see--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosner.

speaker: interviewer
text: His first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But the first name-- he died. He was only 30 years old. So you understand, he always said he want to see me as they should have children, but it was too late, of course, when he was 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: But so the whole family treated these people of the sawmills-- I mean, it was a revolution. They came and stood in front of our house because they used to pull out people and beat them up from the iron car. And those were even punks. You know, guys like Hitler Youth, you know? Who stood in front of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The workers from the sawmill came, like whatever they had in their hand, and they would guard them so nobody can pull us out, so no harm will be done to the whole family. And then, again I have you see, this is how it's happened when you behave and your know how to talk and how to treat people. And that was very important to the Bronsteins as a whole, because then people, how you-- it didn't matter what they are, or they are Greek Orthodox, or they are Jews or whatever they are. That was part of the total religion. You have to respect them 100%. There is no buts.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your citizenship at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I lived? It was Romanian. I was born in Vienna, Austria. So, naturally, you're Austrian. But after you live and you have some school there, then it's citizenship Romanian. I never had-- I don't recall a Romanian passport or whatever, but that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know the truth about the extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The extermination camp started in the '40s or '41. We were still in Romania. In 1941, we were still there. Because, as I said, we had, I think-- not I think. They were German engineers were sent in because they needed the material to build up. They were about 200 kilometer from there, over there, to the Russian front. So they had to build up their hangars to improvise for the airplanes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, therefore, these German engineers, they want us all the times to go over them because they were new. So go to the sawmill and produce and do. And they didn't do nothing to you. Nobody did anything to you. They just let you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to watch your steps. You no go out nights. You stay. You keep calm and that's all. But they didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember they did one night. Not the German engineers, the Romanian. What they did? It was a nice big synagogue and that was in Vatra Dornei, and I mentioned that was that other city for where we went. Vatra. V-A-T-R-A. Dornei. There was a big shul, a synagogue. So one night they took all of the men, all the men-- they came. That was not to do. They took all the men to the shul in the synagogue. Again, there was a mountain there. It was in the foot of the mountain. And on top of the mountain, the Romanian guards put down machine guns and they were rumored that they want to machine gun everybody in that thing, in the shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what's happening is this. That the engineers, the German engineers, found out what's happening and they had naturally influence over the Romanian, because they were their allies in a way. It was not the war yet. We are talking now 1940, 1941, actually. '39 when they broke out Germany, Poland. But it was 1941 when they took us. But the engineers, they came, and not only we the Bronstein's were released, but the whole Jewish community based on us said, you let them go. We need them. We have to finish. Now, at that time we didn't know what was awaiting for us later on. So we thought, hey, we are saved by messiah. But that was a very short dream, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened, we kept doing that. We kept going to the summons. Just follow your job. Do what you have to do, whatever anybody had to do. And when we kept your holidays and everything, cows, and continued the school and whatever it is. Right? And there was, of course, with the Bronstein family-- and I'm sure there is somewhere in the history-- it got to be-- I'm positive of that, not I'm positive that it either is in the Holocaust Museum in the archives, in Washington or something-- one of the son-in-laws of the Bronstein's-- she was a Bronstein, the girl, also. Like from an uncle, a daughter. Her name was [? Lutzi, ?] [? Lutzi ?] Bronstein. She married a fellow by the name Natan Clipper. Natan Clipper was known even in the ghetto down where we came. Through him was saved-- he had great-- a very high intelligent fellow. They were three brothers. One of the brothers, I think, after the war he went to South America in the furniture business. But Clipper, Natan Clipper, because he also knew the whole business-- everybody who married in or whatever it is was put together in the sawmill, because it was big industry. So Clipper naturally, the son-in-law, the whole thing, whole family affair. But then came finally in 1941-- early '41. It wasn't [INAUDIBLE]-- came a general from Germany with some adjutant to check out these German engineers which I was mentioning. And then Natan invited them to his house. Also everybody lived very nice. Invited him, treated him nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they became close friends. And you ask what's happened, when what's happened, very simple. Because in 1941, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], when they gave one hour notice to the whole Jewish community, take your suitcase and walk to the freight cars, you know, to the train, about 10 kilometer, 10 miles. Walk and leave the key in your houses. Those-- yeah? Did you get any warning that you were going to be told to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. As you came out from the shul, you heard over there-- you know, in those days, it's not like you have it here. They put a-- it's known that that was the smaller city, Vatra Dornei, there were some peasants. They had the little drum. In the center of the city, they drum. And they announce, all the Jews, one hour. Take the suitcase. Leave the key. You will be shot on the spot if you don't leave the key in the house. So we thought, sure, make it easy. You don't have to break the doors or anything. Take one suitcase, not the carts, and move to the freight car. There was no warning. However, the night before [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], then we find out. Here's the attack warning. Natan Clipper got a warning from this German general that all the Jews will be packed up and put them on the march. So what he did to Natan-- and don't forget, his father and brothers and mother and the father-in-law, Bronstein, my uncle, you know, no help. But he had one thing. They told him and they said, pack your wife and two children and take off. Otherwise-- OK, only him. No, you know, you taking from--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took off to Bucharest. That was the capital of Romania. They let him. The general himself saw to it. I mean, we found that out later, you know. We didn't know nothing. We wondered when everybody was in the cattle-- everybody's said, where's Natan? Where's [? Lutzi? ?] You know, there was all the family. No. The brothers were there. The in-laws were there. The mothers. Everybody was there. What's happening? So we was even worried, because sometimes, you never know, somebody got mad, shoot them. You know, kill him with his wife and two children. That's all was in our mind as they send us. And we didn't know from him a couple of years. And then as it goes on, that big, sad story, we found out that he was then in Bucharest. And because to that general-- see? There was still Romanian government, but the Germans had total influence naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent special soldiers, German soldiers-- the general-- into the Jewish ghetto in Russia to give to the Bronsteins whatever they could help. He sent some money so he could buy, you know, from the farmers-- you know, buy some flour, buy some potatoes. That what Natan Clipper did. I know that Clipper is-- I read the book and I know that he is someone that was-- [? Lutzi ?], one of my first cousins. And through him, a lot of these people in Mogilev, they could at least survive when they have something to eat. And I mentioned Mogilev, that is a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Clipper survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, as far as I know, one brother. I think. And he was supposed to be in South America in the furniture business. That's all, I think. I couldn't vouch. But Clipper did not survive. His wife survived because she was not in the ghettos. I said she was in Bucharest. His wife survived. I saw her then, my cousin-- she was a Bronstein-- in Israel. She was then in Israel. And his two children. The girls, I wouldn't recognize them today. They're both in the United States. The one is married. The daughter is married to a representative in the United Nations again. He's a representative from the United Nations. And the other one is a professor at Princeton University from this, from Natan Clipper, the kids. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when your family started out to the train station with your one suitcase?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you. I was-- I felt in a way-- because what they were telling-- look, you're 150 or so miles from the Russian war zone. We're going save you. We don't want you to be-- because airplanes were coming over. They bomb. They do all the thing. So we're going evacuate you and send you to a safe place. That was all the time. Then everybody found out that even Auschwitz when they walked in, it was saying, 'Arbeit Macht Frei,' which means, if you work, makes you happy. And that was a camouflage for the people. They're going to save your life. They're going to do that. So you march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe them at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believed them. But there was one way-- to get killed on the spot, or just march and go into the wagon. But I will tell you, I for one-- and, again, I don't want to be the hero, but I for one felt like something's got to give. And if you want to know later on, I'm going to tell you what I did. I probably wouldn't be alive today, but maybe I have higher up a little luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you got to the train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Board the train. Board these cattle wagon. Get them all in there. And then on each wagon, they put up a soldier. But those were Romanian soldiers, not Germans soldiers. It was Romanian. And the Romanian-- probably some-- not probably. Germany could do the same thing [INAUDIBLE]. But the Romanian, if you put something in their hand, and they call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means you give them something-- pay-off. They left the door open so you could get air, because certainly was no air conditioning the cattle, whatever it is. And they didn't mind. And we had over there a baker in that city. He put a whole little van filled up with bread and brought it and gave everybody a bread. And the Jews, excuse me the expression, that was my personal opinion-- if my parents, my grandparents, and all the rest could read my mind at that time what I really felt, they would throw me off the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I thought that all of these, all of them-- including my parents, grandparents, and the whole community-- they were crazy. Why? Because they took along a Torah and they put it down and they were dancing in the wagon. You know? In the wagon. Dancing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it wasn't a dance hall, naturally, as the train pulled. And singing Hebrew songs. Because they were promised they'd just go to work. They will save their life. That was their part. But, you see, we had younger cousins, my younger cousins, who went with us from my uncles. You know, I told you one had 18 children-- girls, boys, the whole thing. I remember some of the names. We were all in accord. Hey, bad things is going to happen to us. It's not going-- But the older, they were dancing and praying and enjoying themselves. Hey, they're going to work. And that's why I said, if they could read our thoughts, they would say we are revolutionaries or something. And so we start going for about five days. We passed through Chernovitz. That is the big city, again, where I was before, where I started. And from there, the transport also stopped coming. And we arrived on the river of Transnistria, which it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any local people on the way as the train was going for these five days?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you mean, local people?

speaker: interviewer
text: Non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The train didn't stop for, you will find, except at the station. They were all the friends. And, as I mentioned before, even the Postmaster General of my close friend were to make Kiddish in our house. And so they all came. Some had, I call it, crocodile tears. I call it. Even some of them cry. Karl, they embrace with Karl, my father. I'm so sorry. Try to apologize. But, again, I couldn't even-- you know, his son came. Because his son, by coincidence, went to high school with me from the-- but somehow our younger cousins, they're my age, we felt that this is toward the end. It's nothing going to that. The older people believed, well, there is a god and God would help us. God did help, in a way, because some, very few survived. But I believe there was more to it. Some people had to do really. And maybe some people don't like the word revolt, like it was Warsaw ghetto revolt or whatever. They don't like the word. So they didn't like the word. They paid dearly with their life. Maybe some of them would more survivors or so. I didn't believe-- I believed strictly in that. But, naturally, when I was six years old, I was trained with the Betar. And the Betar told you, have a prayer and keep a grenade and a shotgun in your right hand and defend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your skin is very expensive. Jewish skin became the most expensive. You cannot expend on that at a Jew skin. There's just no more. It doesn't exist. You know? Because you cannot how many Jews are left in the total world. How old were you in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 19 years old. 1920. Something that age. I was born in '21.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the train stopped? Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take you on the border. On this side, it's called Bessarabia. And then get the Dniester. This is like the Mississippi River. This is one of the big river. And if you cross this river, you are in the Ukraine. The Ukraine, of course, and a big part of Russia was already occupied by the Nazis, by the Germans, and Romanian. But the Romanian took just the lower part, just a little part, you know, like what you call-- see the Nazi army, the tank and armor, they advanced. The Romanian were like you saw the background occupation. Like law and order, what you want to call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was Mogilev. Podolski. But they kept you by the thousands of people arrived, like every hour and hour from all over, you know, from these parts, on the Dniester, on the border, on that river.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was rainy. And I remember deep mud. And they couldn't take you across the Dniester. They only had like a pontoon-- you know, like you pull with wires. There was no boat get across the river. And many people fall in. Maybe they were better. They were dead then right there. They never arrived on the other side. And this is how we're shipping.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they shipped them only during the day, and there were tens of thousands of people across the Dniester. So what's happened? They had to keep them overnight in the mud with your one suitcase. And you start freezing in the wet and dirty. So what's happen again? The peasants. And you had all of a sudden loud voices and crying. You know why? Because the peasants were over running, grabbing your last suitcase, what you have, whatever you had, and take it away and beat you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when the train arrived, these trains, they were not on a station. They were on an embankment. High, real high. And you had to jump. They opened the door and the Romanian was saying, hey, you dirty Jew dogs. Jump. Young kids, we can jump. But the older people, they could hardly walk. So they didn't care. They fell over. They were half-dead. They hit them with the [INAUDIBLE] of the gun. Now, I decided I have to say that. I decide if I jump, I have to jump and do something. And as they were staying, the Romanian gendarme-- gendarme, they call them-- I jumped to a soldier right in his neck. And you know, because a big jump-- deep. And I jump, and he fell over with me. He fell naturally, the big tank. And then he right way took the gun, you know, reversed the gun, and he hit me a couple times in the back. And they schlepped me away. Everybody was crying. My mother, my father, the whole-- oy,  gevalt! They take him. They schlepped me away and they threw me in a little dungeon and there were two more people in there. I'll never forget that. That's before we crossed the Dniester. And there were already controlled. There were German soldiers, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they walked in and they said to us three young people-- I don't know who they were, because from all over they schlepped in there. But they were Jews. And they said, you three dogs. You will be executed. Three dogs. We'll execute you. OK, one after the other. So one of the-- from our people, they came and they start saying, what do you want from these guys? You maybe don't know. In [INAUDIBLE], in Germany, they did that, they released from all the crazy house the mental disturbed people. They released them. They don't know what they're doing. So what, are you going to shoot mentally disturbed? They're all crazy. Let them go with them. They go across the Dniester. Who was it that told this to the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the people from the community, our Jewish people. He said, they're mental. We had the mentally retarded hospital. They released them. These are one of them, you [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are you going to do to them? Because they say, hey, jumped our soldier. You know, I jumped him. He fell over. You know? It's an insult.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was my first thing. And that was one of my first escapades. Because, again, I saw I wasn't Elijah. I wasn't a prophet. Nothing smart about me. But I'd been introduced as a child to defend as much I can, and I saw doom days right in front. Unfortunately, my parents or other people, they maybe couldn't see. God [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they say. God would help. He did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you got across the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I crossed the river. So you had to find shelter. That was Mogilev-Podolski. You had to find shelter. And if you find shelter, it was bombed. So if you find something which has a hole in the ceiling or so, you crawl in there. So you stay. Now, you stay. Not for long, because the Romanian gendarme did not want to give you peace. So if you were there, they grabbed you, whatever they had. And they start what they called transport. Send you up the hill. Mogilev-Podolski is on the foot of cliffs, cliffs over the Dniester. And they filled up the cliffs with Jewish bodies as they schlepped them across. They threw them into the cliffs. That's all. That's all they want. They didn't have what to take it. But that's all. So it was a running match.
